steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The blank falls between the verb "appear" and the noun phrase "US currency." The word "on" is a preposition that must connect directly to its object — no punctuation may separate them.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'semicolon before "on"': A semicolon separates the preposition from what follows, breaking the required direct connection to the object. ✗
  • Choice B — 'comma before "on"': A comma between the preposition and its object is also grammatically illegal. ✗
  • Choice C — 'colon before "on"': A colon here similarly wedges punctuation between preposition and object. ✗
  • Choice D — 'no punctuation, "on US currency" uninterrupted': "On" flows directly into "US currency" with no intervening punctuation, which is the only grammatically correct option. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
